name: RABBIT-CAGE-BUG-97
title: Revert bypass-human-approval subcommand rename back to human-approval (natural-language misparse)
description:
BACKLOG-31 renamed the human-approval subcommand to bypass-human-approval to achieve semantic parallelism with bypass-permissions (both true=bypass-on). The rename was justified on system-centric grounds (parallel semantics) but it introduced a worse user-facing failure mode: natural-language 'turn off X' creates a double-negative trap.

Observed misparse incident (2026-05-21, ws40):
- User instruction: 'turn off human approval and enter bypass permission mode'
- User intent: skip the approval gate (bypass ACTIVE)
- ws40 parsed: 'turn off [the subcommand named bypass-human-approval]' → bypass-human-approval false → gate ACTIVE
- Result: opposite of user intent. ws40 ran in non-autonomous mode until corrected.

Root cause: 'turn off X' where X itself starts with 'bypass-' has two defensible parses:
  - Literal: turn off [human approval] → bypass ON → true
  - Variable: turn off [bypass-human-approval] → bypass OFF → false
ws40 picked the variable parse. Both reads are reasonable; the naming itself is the bug.

Pre-rename naming worked for natural language:
  /rabbit-config human-approval true|false  (true=gate active, false=bypassed)
  'turn off human approval' → human-approval false ✓ (correct)
  'enable human approval' → human-approval true ✓ (correct)

The semantic asymmetry with bypass-permissions is real but acceptable: bypass-permissions is pinned to Claude Code's API value 'bypassPermissions', so its naming is externally constrained. human-approval is internally controlled and should optimize for natural-language clarity.

Fix (essentially the inverse of BACKLOG-31):

1. rabbit-config.py: rename subparser bypass-human-approval back to human-approval. Invert value semantics so 'human-approval false' writes the bypass marker and 'human-approval true' deletes it.
2. SKILL.md: update CLI surface section + frontmatter description.
3. Spec invariants: find current Inv numbers via grep (post-renumber), revert wording.
4. Cross-feature refs in rabbit-feature SKILL.md (Step 4 bypass warning text mentions revoke command), tdd-subagent dispatch script preamble (if it still mentions the command), rabbit-print registry entry text for human-approval-bypass and dispatch-bypass-note (both mention the revoke command).
5. Marker file .rabbit-human-approval-bypass UNCHANGED (it names the marker, not the subcommand).
6. Banner text 'HUMAN APPROVAL BYPASS ACTIVE' UNCHANGED.
7. Hard rename (no coexistence window) — same path as BACKLOG-31's hard rename.
8. Regression test: explicitly assert that 'turn off human approval' style intent maps to 'human-approval false' subcommand → bypass marker WRITTEN. Belt-and-suspenders against future re-rename attempts.
9. Update BACKLOG-31's close note in CHANGELOG (or wherever recorded) to reference this revert and the lessons learned.

Lesson for future naming decisions:
- Test with conversational/natural-language phrasing, not just system invariants
- System-centric parallelism is a code-aesthetics value; user-facing natural-language clarity is operationally more important
- When a subcommand name leads with a negating verb ('bypass-', 'disable-'), 'turn off X' creates double-negative ambiguity

Acceptance:
- /rabbit-config human-approval works (true=gate active, false=bypass active)
- /rabbit-config bypass-human-approval is REJECTED (hard rename) with helpful error pointing at new name
- All cross-feature references reverted
- Regression test asserts the natural-language mapping
- All 7 feature suites pass on main

history.note: TDD cycle complete + cross-feature ripples (chore commit c9afe5e0)
